perturbation.original_text: Distributor has the right to cancel, reschedule or modify all or any portion of a purchase order that has been accepted by Xplore at no cost to Distributor only if such cancellation or modification request is made within [*****] business days of PO acceptance or rescheduling request is made at least [*****] business days prior to the most current Estimated Shipping Date for that purchase order.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor may request to cancel, reschedule or modify a purchase order, subject to Xplore's approval. Xplore may impose a cancellation or modification fee.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the guaranteed right to cancel or modify purchase orders within a specified timeframe, and instead making it subject to Xplore's approval and potential fees, introduces a legal risk. This contradicts consumer protection laws regarding fair and transparent business practices, specifically concerning order modifications and cancellations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 2310 - Remedies in case of failure of warranty obligations; informal dispute settlement procedures
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S. Code § 2310 mandates fair and transparent procedures for resolving disputes, including those related to product orders and warranties. By removing the distributor's guaranteed right to cancel or modify orders within a reasonable timeframe and introducing potential fees, the modified text creates a condition that could unfairly disadvantage the distributor, potentially leading to disputes without clear and fair resolution procedures, thus contradicting the spirit and intent of this law.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial action with respect to such product in the event that such product fails to meet the specifications set forth in the undertaking, which written affirmati
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the distributor's right to cancel or modify orders within a specified timeframe, replacing it with a condition subject to Xplore's approval and potential fees. The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 2310, which mandates fair and transparent procedures for resolving disputes. While the provided snippet focuses on consumer product warranties (15 USC Ch. 50), §2310 falls under this chapter and addresses remedies in consumer disputes, suggesting the change could unfairly disadvantage the distributor. The original text provided a defined right, which is now replaced with a discretionary approval process potentially imposing fees, thus weakening the distributor's position and potentially leading to disputes without clear resolution, contradicting the spirit of fair consumer practices within product warranties.

perturbation.original_text: Each purchase order placed by Distributor, as well as each invoice sent by Xplore, shall be governed by the terms of this Addendum and the Distribution Agreement and any additional or different terms within the purchase order or invoice shall have no effect.
perturbation.changed_text: Each purchase order placed by Distributor, as well as each invoice sent by Xplore, shall be governed by the terms of this Addendum and the Distribution Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'any additional or different terms within the purchase order or invoice shall have no effect' creates ambiguity and legal risk. Without this clause, Xplore can potentially insert terms into invoices that contradict the original agreement, undermining the distributor's rights and creating compliance issues. This contradicts laws about contract modifications needing mutual consent.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-207 addresses the battle of the forms and requires that additional terms in acceptance or confirmation (like invoices) must be explicitly agreed upon to become part of the contract. By omitting the clause that prevents such additional terms from taking effect, the altered text allows Xplore to unilaterally introduce new terms via invoices, which undermines the distributor's contractual rights and violates the principles of mutual agreement required under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-207. Additional Terms in Acceptance or Confirmation. (1) A definite and seasonable expression of acceptance or a written confirmation which is sent within a reasonable time operates as an acceptance even though it states terms additional to or different from those offered or agreed upon, unless acceptance is expressly made conditional on assent to the additional or different terms. (2) The additional terms are to be construed as proposals for addition to the contract . Between merchants such terms become part of the contract unless: (a) the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer; (b) they materially alter it; or (c) notification of objection to them has already been given or is given within a reasonable time after notice of them is received. (3) Conduct by both parties which recognizes the existence of a contract is sufficient to establish a contract for sale although the writings of the parties do not otherwise establish a contract. In such case the terms of the particular contract consist of those terms on which the writings of the parties agree, together with any supplementary terms incorporated under any other provisions of this Act. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'any additional or different terms within the purchase order or invoice shall have no effect' directly contradicts the principles outlined in UCC § 2-207, specifically regarding additional terms in acceptance or confirmation. The original text explicitly prevents unilateral modifications via purchase orders or invoices. By removing this, Xplore can introduce new terms that could materially alter the agreement without explicit consent from the distributor. UCC § 2-207(2) states that such additional terms are 'proposals for addition to the contract' and, between merchants, become part of the contract unless certain conditions are met (e.g., the offer expressly limits acceptance to the terms of the offer, they materially alter it, or notification of objection is given). The original clause preemptively addresses these conditions, ensuring the distributor's original terms prevail. Removing it allows the potential for Xplore to impose terms, creating a legally significant contradiction by undermining the original agreement and the distributor's protection against unilateral changes.

perturbation.original_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales. Xplore acknowledges that from time to time, Distributor's inventory levels may fall below the thirty (30) days goal that is agreed upon by both Parties. If inventory levels fall below the thirty (30) day goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore, upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor shall use commercially reasonable efforts to maintain thirty (30) days of stock in Distributor's inventory to support sales. If inventory levels fall below the thirty (30) day goal for more than sixty (60) consecutive days, Xplore may, at its discretion, replenish the stock.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'Xplore acknowledges that from time to time, Distributor's inventory levels may fall below the thirty (30) days goal that is agreed upon by both Parties' and 'upon written notice to Distributor, shall replenish the stock to an amount agreed by both Parties' significantly weakens the distributor's position. By omitting acknowledgement and making replenishment discretionary, the changed text permits Xplore to avoid obligations to maintain stock levels, potentially harming the distributor's business. This creates a legal issue, potentially violating implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 1-304 imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. By removing the acknowledgement of potential inventory shortfalls and making stock replenishment discretionary, Xplore could potentially act in bad faith by failing to support the distributor's sales efforts, thus undermining the purpose of the distribution agreement and violating this section of the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts UCC § 1-304, which imposes an obligation of good faith in the performance and enforcement of contracts. The original text acknowledged potential inventory shortfalls and mandated Xplore to replenish stock, maintaining a level of support for the distributor's sales efforts. By removing this acknowledgment and making replenishment discretionary, the changed text allows Xplore to potentially act in bad faith by not supporting the distributor. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states: 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' The removal of Xplore's obligation to replenish the stock directly undermines the distributor's ability to maintain sales, thereby contradicting the obligation of good faith as required by the UCC.
